.ddx/beads.jsonl:186:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As an operator, I want a single view that shows every place my agents can run ã endpoint providers and subprocess harnesses alike ã with status, recent token usage, and quota utilization. When I click into one, I see a 7-day trend so I can tell if I'm about to run out.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Deliverable 1 ã Unified view.**
   - New `harnessStatuses` GraphQL resolver returns subprocess harnesses (claude, codex, gemini, agent) with: name, kind=`harness`, reachable, detail, lastCheckedAt, defaultForProfile.
   - Existing `providerStatuses` shape gains `kind=\"endpoint\"` and `lastCheckedAt`.
   - UI page loads within 500ms on a warm cache ã it renders from last-known status **first**, then patches rows live as async probes return. Playwright asserts the table is interactive within 500ms of navigation and that row status patches within 5s of the probe completing.
   - Subprocess harnesses claude and codex **appear in the table** on a machine where their binaries are installed, with correct reachable status. Integration test.

2. **Deliverable 2 ã Usage + quota fields (stubbable).**
   - Per-row fields `usage` and `quota` as described. All fields nullable.
   - For endpoint providers, `usage.tokensUsedLast24h` and `usage.tokensUsedLastHour` are computed from the sessions index (fixture: ãË1k session rows covering the last 24h). Unit test asserts correctness on a seeded window.
   - For subprocess harnesses that expose rate-limit headers (claude, codex), parsed headers populate `quota.ceilingTokens`, `quota.ceilingWindowSeconds`, `quota.remaining`, `quota.resetAt`. Parser tested with captured header fixtures from each harness.
   - For harnesses/providers that expose nothing, fields remain null and the UI renders `ã` / "not reported"; no fabricated data.
   - Utilization bar renders when both `usage` and `quota.ceilingTokens` are present. Sparkline renders when ãË6 hourly buckets of usage are available.

3. **Deliverable 3 ã Trend detail.**
   - Detail route `/nodes/.../providers/[name]` shows 7-day and 30-day tokens/hour + requests/hour time series, with quota ceiling overlay when known.
   - Buckets computed server-side from the sessions index; the client receives ãÊ 200 data points per series.
   - "Projected to hit quota in ~X" callout uses last-24h slope only. Renders only when `quota.ceilingTokens` is known and the last-24h slope is non-zero positive; otherwise absent.
   - Playwright asserts the detail route renders with seeded usage and the projection text appears for a fixture that has a ceiling + ascending usage.

4. **Perf posture.**
   - Fixture for perf test: ãË1,000 session index rows covering the last 30 days with usage distributed across ãË5 endpoints/harnesses. Per standing directive in `ddx-9ce6842a`.
   - Targets: unified list p95 ãÊ 200ms HTTP (excluding async probes); trend detail p95 ãÊ 400ms HTTP on the fixture.
   - If in-place aggregation cannot hit the detail-view p95 on a 10,000-row stretch fixture, pause and raise the DB-substrate decision with the user per the standing directive ã do not silently switch.

5. **Design note.**
   - One-pager in `docs/helix/02-design/` before implementation recording the page-rename decision, the row model (common fields, kind-specific fields), async-probe strategy, and the quota source per harness/provider. PR links to it.

6. **No regressions.**
   - `ddx agent providers`, `ddx agent doctor`, `ddx agent list`, `ddx agent check` CLI commands continue to work unchanged.
   - `defaultRouteStatus` query remains.

7. **Cross-references.**
   - Depends on `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index) for usage aggregation. If that bead is not yet landed when this work starts, ship Deliverable 1 standalone with usage/quota stubbed as "not available," and re-open the remaining deliverables when the index is ready.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","closing_commit_sha":"7ef7a4b1c517483485821f99aec728292bbe83a1","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:48:37.871527387Z","description":"## Observed

Three related issues on `/nodes/.../providers`:

1. **Loads slowly.** The page fires two GraphQL queries on mount ã `providerStatuses` (which probes every configured provider for live connectivity) and `defaultRouteStatus` (which resolves the default route). Both are synchronous and serialized only by `Promise.all`. Each provider probe is a real network call.
2. **Missing harnesses.** The pane shows API-endpoint providers (qwen, minimax, etc. ã anything wired through the service with an endpoint). It does not show subprocess-invoked harnesses like `claude` (Claude Code) or `codex`. That is accurate to the current `providerStatuses` resolver (`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_providers.go:15-50`, which calls `svc.ListProviders`) but not what an operator wants: the operator thinks of "where their agent work can run," which includes both endpoint providers AND subprocess harnesses.
3. **No quota visibility.** Operator need: see current token utilization rate + quota utilization per harness/provider, trend it, and use the trend to decide whether to throttle before hitting a cap.

## Root cause by area

- **Slow load:** `svc.ListProviders` probes every configured provider for liveness. Probes are serial within the service call, and the whole query blocks page render.
- **Missing harnesses:** Subprocess harnesses (claude, codex, gemini) are first-class in the CLI (`ddx agent list`, `ddx agent doctor`, `ddx agent check`) but are not surfaced via `providerStatuses`. They have their own status concepts ã binary installed, credentials configured, rate-limit headroom ã that don't map cleanly onto the endpoint-provider model.
- **No quota:** No existing data source captures rolling token usage vs quota. Quota information varies wildly by harness: Claude Code has message / session headroom concepts from response headers; codex has rate-limit headers; endpoint providers have API quota per key. There is no unified representation today.

## Proposed direction

Three deliverables, sequenced from easy to hard.

### Deliverable 1 ã Unified "Agent endpoints" view (renames/rescopes the page)

- Rename the surface from "Providers" to **"Agent endpoints"** (or similar ã confirm copy in the design note). The domain object is "a place where agent work can run," not "an API provider."
- Merge the data: `providerStatuses` stays as the source for endpoint providers. A new `harnessStatuses` resolver (read from `svc.ListHarnesses` / existing `ddx agent list` + `ddx agent doctor` machinery) surfaces subprocess harnesses. Page renders both in one table with a `kind` column (`endpoint` | `harness`) ã or two tables if design prefers.
- Status fields common to both: name, kind, reachable (bool), detail (text), last-checked-at, default-for-profile (set of profile names).
- Asynchronous liveness. The page renders an instant first paint from cached status; liveness probes fire in background and patch rows as they return. No synchronous probe-wall on load.

### Deliverable 2 ã Token & quota fields

- Extend the status payload (per row) with: `usage { tokensUsedLastHour, tokensUsedLast24h, requestsLastHour, requestsLast24h }` and `quota { ceilingTokens?, ceilingWindowSeconds?, remaining?, resetAt? }`. Any field may be null if the provider/harness doesn't expose it.
- Data sources:
  - **Endpoint providers:** aggregate from the sessions index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`) filtered to the last 1h / 24h, grouped by provider. Quota ceilings come from any headers we capture during calls, plus optional static config.
  - **Subprocess harnesses:** same sessions-index aggregation for usage. Quota ã parse rate-limit headers from the harness output where available (Claude Code, codex both emit them). For harnesses that don't, leave quota null and render "not reported."
- Row gains two small inline displays: utilization bar (used / ceiling for the quota window) and a 7-day sparkline of tokens/hour.

### Deliverable 3 ã Trend view

- Click into a row ã detail page showing: 7-day and 30-day time-series of tokens/hour and requests/hour, with the quota ceiling overlaid. Aggregations come from the sessions index, bucketed server-side to avoid shipping raw rows.
- Simple "projected to hit quota in ~X hours/days" callout computed from the slope of the last 24h ã enough to answer "are we on a trend to run out?" without building a forecasting system.

## Cross-bead dependencies

- Depends on `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index) for any usage-rate data. If that bead isn't landed when this work starts, the usage/quota features stub to "not available" and ship purely with the unified-view rename + async liveness.
- Fixture / perf guidance: per the standing directive recorded in `ddx-9ce6842a`, any aggregation here is tested against ãË1k session records. Attempt: 20260425T185929-073fe149 (model: claude opus 4.7)

DECISION: no_changes ã bead implementation is substantially complete in tree at HEAD; remaining gap requires architectural decision that must be raised with user.

== What is already in tree at base 7ef7a4b1 ==

The cumulative work from prior attempts (commits including 3102f0da, c334d94e, f912445f, and others tagged [ddx-23978824]) is merged and present:

AC 1 ã Unified view + async load:
  cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_providers.go
    - HarnessStatuses resolver returns rows with kind=HARNESS, lastCheckedAt, defaultForProfile
    - ProviderStatuses returns rows with kind=ENDPOINT, lastCheckedAt
    - First-paint reads from cachedProviderRows; refreshProviderStatuses fires async
    - Synchronous probe path bounded to 500ms via context timeout
  cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/providers/+page.svelte
    - Renders providerStatuses + harnessStatuses in unified table with kind column
    - defaultRouteStatus issued as a separate parallel query (does not block table)
    - 2.5s poll interval patches rows after first paint (within 5s AC budget)
    - data-testid="agent-endpoints-table", endpoint-row-{name}, endpoint-kind-{name}, endpoint-reachable-{name}

AC 2 ã Usage + quota + sparkline:
  cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_providers.go:480 (buildUsage)
    - Returns nil ProviderUsage when all counts are zero ã no fabricated "0/0" rows
  cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_providers.go:421 (buildSparkline)
    - 24-element hourly token totals, returns nil when <6 non-zero buckets
  cli/internal/agent/ratelimit_headers.go
    - ParseRateLimitHeaders for claude/codex header families (with header fixtures in tests)
  cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_providers.go (harnessRateLimitCache + RecordHarnessRateLimit)
    - Bridge from captured rate-limit signal -> ProviderQuota fields (verified by TestHarnessQuotaFromCapturedRateLimitSignal)
  +page.svelte:228-237 (sparkline rendering) and :408-419 (utilization bar)

AC 3 ã Trend detail:
  cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/providers/[name]/+page.svelte
    - Renders 7d hourly + 30d 4h-bucketed series via providerTrend GraphQL query
    - Quota ceiling rendered as red dashed overlay (data-testid ceiling-overlay-7d/30d), NOT trailing text
    - Projection callout (data-testid projection-callout) rendered only when ceiling+positive slope present
  cli/internal/server/graphql resolver providerTrend exists with bucketed server-side aggregation

AC 4 ã Perf fixtures:
  cli/internal/server/graphql/providers_unified_test.go
    - TestProviderStatusesPerfFixture: 1,200 rows across 5 endpoints + 2 harnesses, p95 <=150ms (200ms HTTP budget)
    - TestProviderTrendPerfFixture: 1,200 rows across 30 days, exercises detail-view path

AC 5 ã Design note:
  docs/helix/02-design/agent-endpoints-unified-view.md exists.

AC 6 ã No regressions:
  go test ./internal/server/graphql/... and ./internal/agent/... pass under -short.

== What is NOT in tree (and why no commit was made this pass) ==

1. RecordHarnessRateLimit is never called from a non-test code path.
   - The function and the cache that bridges parsed headers -> ProviderQuota are wired and unit-tested end-to-end via TestHarnessQuotaFromCapturedRateLimitSignal.
   - But no production call site invokes ParseRateLimitHeaders + RecordHarnessRateLimit after a real claude/codex invocation. grep -rn "RecordHarnessRateLimit\|ParseRateLimitHeaders" outside of resolver_providers.go and *_test.go returns nothing.
   - Why this is non-trivial: claude-code and codex are subprocess CLIs. They do NOT return HTTP-style headers from a Go http.Client whose Header map can be passed straight to ParseRateLimitHeaders. To wire this for real, one of:
       (a) the harness adapters must capture the upstream HTTP response headers from inside the subprocess (requires Claude Code / codex protocol changes ã not in our control), OR
       (b) we add a stdout/stderr-format parser that scrapes rate-limit fields the harnesses emit on completion (Claude Code emits a JSON line with usage; codex prints a rate-limit summary), and feed those into a synthetic header map or a new structured input to a sibling RecordHarness* function.
   - Option (b) is the realistic path but it is a meaningful adapter change. Per the standing directive recorded in memory (multi-model plan review for non-trivial plans, plus the standing "raise architectural decisions to the user" directive for substrate choices), I should NOT silently pick (b) ã the user should weigh in on whether to scrape stdout/stderr per-harness or wait for upstream protocol features.

2. agent-endpoints.e2e.ts still mocks providerTrend instead of fetching from a server-seeded fixture.
   - The Go-side equivalent (TestProviderTrendProjectionFromSeededUsageAndCeiling) seeds usage + a real rate-limit-derived ceiling and asserts the projection field, satisfying the AC's "seeded fixture proves projection text appears" intent at the resolver layer.
   - Replacing the Playwright mock requires standing up the real Go server inside the Playwright config with seeded session-index files, which is an e2e-infrastructure change rather than a feature change. Worth doing, but again should be planned, not improvised in this attempt.

== Recommendation for next iteration ==

Open a small follow-up bead specifically for "wire harness rate-limit capture from claude/codex subprocess output" with the user choosing between options (a) and (b) above. That keeps this bead's quota-display surface honest (it works the moment any caller records a signal), and isolates the adapter work behind its own scope.

The reviewer's repeated REVIEW:BLOCK with "Diff scope wrong" message indicates that the reviewer is being shown only the post-merge bookkeeping commits (.ddx/executions/.../result.json) instead of the actual implementation diffs. That is an execute-bead evidence-extraction problem, not an implementation problem ã the implementation is in the tree. Re-extracting at the documented base..result_rev range as the prior block notes already instruct should let the next review see the work that's there.

== Verification commands ==

  cd cli && go test -short ./internal/server/graphql/... ./internal/agent/...
  cd cli && go test ./internal/server/graphql/ -run TestProviderStatusesPerfFixture
  cd cli && go test ./internal/server/graphql/ -run TestHarnessQuotaFromCapturedRateLimitSignal
  cd cli && go test ./internal/server/graphql/ -run TestProviderTrendProjectionFromSeededUsageAndCeiling

    - TestProviderTrendPerfFixture: 1,200 rows across 30 days, exercises detail-view path

AC 5 ã Design note:
  docs/helix/02-design/agent-endpoints-unified-view.md exists.

AC 6 ã No regressions:
  go test ./internal/server/graphql/... and ./internal/agent/... pass under -short.

== What is NOT in tree (and why no commit was made this pass) ==

1. RecordHarnessRateLimit is never called from a non-test code path.
   - The function and the cache that bridges parsed headers -> ProviderQuota are wired and unit-tested end-to-end via TestHarnessQuotaFromCapturedRateLimitSignal.
   - But no production call site invokes ParseRateLimitHeaders + RecordHarnessRateLimit after a real claude/codex invocation. grep -rn "RecordHarnessRateLimit\|ParseRateLimitHeaders" outside of resolver_providers.go and *_test.go returns nothing.
   - Why this is non-trivial: claude-code and codex are subprocess CLIs. They do NOT return HTTP-style headers from a Go http.Client whose Header map can be passed straight to ParseRateLimitHeaders. To wire this for real, one of:
       (a) the harness adapters must capture the upstream HTTP response headers from inside the subprocess (requires Claude Code / codex protocol changes ã not in our control), OR
       (b) we add a stdout/stderr-format parser that scrapes rate-limit fields the harnesses emit on completion (Claude Code emits a JSON line with usage; codex prints a rate-limit summary), and feed those into a synthetic header map or a new structured input to a sibling RecordHarness* function.
   - Option (b) is the realistic path but it is a meaningful adapter change. Per the standing directive recorded in memory (multi-model plan review for non-trivial plans, plus the standing "raise architectural decisions to the user" directive for substrate choices), I should NOT silently pick (b) ã the user should weigh in on whether to scrape stdout/stderr per-harness or wait for upstream protocol features.

2. agent-endpoints.e2e.ts still mocks providerTrend instead of fetching from a server-seeded fixture.
   - The Go-side equivalent (TestProviderTrendProjectionFromSeededUsageAndCeiling) seeds usage + a real rate-limit-derived ceiling and asserts the projection field, satisfying the AC's "seeded fixture proves projection text appears" intent at the resolver layer.
   - Replacing the Playwright mock requires standing up the real Go server inside the Playwright config with seeded session-index files, which is an e2e-infrastructure change rather than a feature change. Worth doing, but again should be planned, not improvised in this attempt.

== Recommendation for next iteration ==

Open a small follow-up bead specifically for "wire harness rate-limit capture from claude/codex subprocess output" with the user choosing between options (a) and (b) above. That keeps this bead's quota-display surface honest (it works the moment any caller records a signal), and isolates the adapter work behind its own scope.

The reviewer's repeated REVIEW:BLOCK with "Diff scope wrong" message indicates that the reviewer is being shown only the post-merge bookkeeping commits (.ddx/executions/.../result.json) instead of the actual implementation diffs. That is an execute-bead evidence-extraction problem, not an implementation problem ã the implementation is in the tree. Re-extracting at the documented base..result_rev range as the prior block notes already instruct should let the next review see the work that's there.

== Verification commands ==

  cd cli && go test -short ./internal/server/graphql/... ./internal/agent/...
  cd cli && go test ./internal/server/graphql/ -run TestProviderStatusesPerfFixture
  cd cli && go test ./internal/server/graphql/ -run TestHarnessQuotaFromCapturedRateLimitSignal
  cd cli && go test ./internal/server/graphql/ -run TestProviderTrendProjectionFromSeededUsageAndCeiling

Back button from detail returns to artifacts list with filter/search state preserved.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-05-01T03:34:23Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"3396374","closing_commit_sha":"b62a1ece2366db556af062a4cf366c1ea6c234e5","created_at":"2026-04-30T15:09:22.480634259Z","description":"Build the artifact detail route at /nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/artifacts/:artifactId.

Renderers by mediaType:
- text/markdown: rendered markdown with syntax-highlighted code blocks; intra-repo relative links navigate within UI (same tab, no reload); external links open in new tab.
- image/svg+xml with Mermaid provenance: sanitized inline SVG (DOMPurify); raw download affordance alongside.
- application/vnd.excalidraw+json: read-only Excalidraw embed via hosted iframe (https://excalidraw.com/embed); raw JSON download affordance. Offline fallback: show 'Excalidraw preview requires network' with download only.
- image/*: inline <img> preview; metadata panel; open/download affordance.
- application/pdf: <embed type=application/pdf> with open-file fallback link; file size shown.
- unknown/binary: metadata-only panel with open/download affordance; no attempt to render.

All renderers show: path, mediaType badge, updatedAt, sidecar metadata panel (DDx frontmatter key/value).

.ddx/beads.jsonl:1099:{"acceptance":"FEAT-002, FEAT-013, architecture.md, and TP-002 describe one `ddx server` process managing multiple project roots on one machine with explicit project registry, request scoping, per-project isolation, and migration semantics from the current single-project host","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-10T04:15:08Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"488251","closing_commit_sha":"8e1727512319acb8b916ebbebbccb7607924a9c7","created_at":"2026-04-10T02:22:52.264935134Z","description":"<context-digest>
Review area: ddx server topology. Evidence covers FEAT-002, FEAT-013, architecture.md, TP-002, and the current assumption that one `ddx server` serves exactly one repo.
</context-digest>
Design the first-step multi-project `ddx server` module for a single machine.

## Goals
1. Replace the single-project server assumption with a project registry / project-root collection managed by one server process on one machine
2. Define project-scoped API, MCP, and UI routing semantics so clients can enumerate projects and then operate within one selected project context
3. Define per-project config, cache, and adapter boundaries so one bad project cannot poison other served projects
4. Define how project-scoped worktrees and worker pools are managed under one server process without assuming only one active repository
5. Define migration from today's single-project `ddx server` invocation and data adapters without breaking current local use

## Required outputs
- Project registry/discovery contract
- Request routing and project selection model
- Per-project isolation and failure-mode analysis
- Impact on FEAT-008 UI and TP-002 end-to-end coverage","id":"ddx-efd858c4","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:planning","kind:architecture","area:api","area:agent","area:docs"],"owner":"erik","parent":"ddx-8b6cd40e","priority":0,"spec-id":"FEAT-002","status":"closed","title":"Design one-machine multi-project ddx server topology","updated_at":"2026-04-10T04:54:20.488868062Z"}
.ddx/beads.jsonl:1121:{"acceptance":"FEAT-006, FEAT-002, and FEAT-008 define a structured embedded-agent progress contract for managed runs; the contract names the required phases and fields, distinguishes runtime-only progress from tracked execution evidence, and defines one shared read model for CLI and server/UI consumers","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-14T05:09:51Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1412283","closing_commit_sha":"82ff773b8d56e7a883dd79ba7cd8ad963c322439","created_at":"2026-04-10T22:16:04.952097087Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-f5337613","depends_on_id":"ddx-ffda4fb5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-10T22:20:18Z"}],"description":"<context-digest>
Review area: embedded ddx-agent runtime observability and live worker progress. Evidence covers FEAT-006 execution/evidence ownership, FEAT-002 server activity surfaces, FEAT-008 web UI expectations, execute-loop worker supervision, and the need for DDx-owned progress telemetry beyond coarse session logs when DDx runs the embedded harness.
</context-digest>
Define the progress-event contract for managed embedded-agent runs so DDx can surface meaningful live execution status in the CLI and server/UI.

## Goals
- Define structured phase/heartbeat output for embedded-agent runs (queueing, launch, running, post-checks, landing, preserved, done)
- Define the minimal per-event fields needed for operator visibility and later reporting: worker/project/bead/run identity, phase, elapsed/heartbeat timing, harness/provider/model/profile, and token/cost deltas when available
- Clarify which live progress artifacts remain runtime-only and which summaries/indexes are exposed through DDx-owned APIs and UI surfaces
- Keep provider-native transcript ownership external; DDx progress output should be normalized runtime telemetry, not transcript duplication

## Required spec work
- Update FEAT-006, FEAT-002, and FEAT-008 so embedded-agent progress visibility is an explicit contract rather than an implementation detail
- Clarify how progress events relate to tracked execution artifacts, session logs, and server-managed worker state

## Required implementation planning
- Define the server/API/UI read model for live worker progress and recent phase history
- Define how CLI and UI consume the same structured progress feed without introducing hidden workflow policy","events":[{"actor":"erik","body":"checkpointing dirty worktree: git stash: error: could not write index

Addresses US-081 AC which is specced but unimplemented.

Bugs to fix:
1. Pan/zoom sizing race: D3Graph.svelte uses el.clientWidth at init time which may be 0. Replace with ResizeObserver: set up simulation once observer fires with non-zero dimensions. This also handles window resize.
2. Node click = no handler: nodeGroup has mouseenter/mousemove/mouseleave but no onclick. Add click handler that navigates to /nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/documents/:path (for markdown artifacts) or /artifacts/:artifactId (for other types). Distinguish by mediaType from graph node data.

Viewport persistence:
- Save zoom transform (x, y, k) to component state or URL param (e.g. ?zoom=1.2&pan=120,45) on each zoom/pan end event.
- On Back navigation, restore the saved transform before the D3 effect runs.

New features:
- Filter chips on graph page: filter nodes by mediaType (markdown, image, unknown) and staleness (fresh, stale, missing). Hidden nodes removed from simulation; edges to hidden nodes removed.
- Node color = staleness: green=fresh, amber=stale, red=missing (not dependsOn.length as today).
- 'View document list' link on graph page ã /documents (which redirects to /artifacts).
- 'View in Graph' link on artifact detail page (once ddx-d7d1119d exists) ã /graph with node pre-selected.

.ddx/beads.jsonl:1166:{"acceptance":"FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, and TP-002 all describe the same host+user ddx-server model, including runtime/index storage, worker boundaries, provider dashboarding, replay-backed fixtures, and mandatory Playwright coverage for new UI surfaces","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","created_at":"2026-04-10T22:20:09.416115777Z","description":"<context-digest>
Review area: host+user ddx-server planning stack after the server model evolved beyond the earlier multi-project topology draft. Evidence covers FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, TP-002, the fresh-eyes server review, and the updated requirements for a per-user host daemon, localhost HTTP UI, SQLite-backed runtime/index state, embedded-agent progress visibility, provider dashboarding, replay-backed fixtures, and Playwright coverage for every new UI surface.
</context-digest>
Update the governing server planning docs so they describe the current intended host+user ddx-server model before follow-on server design and implementation beads continue.

## Goals
- Reconcile FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, and TP-002 around a single host+user ddx-server instance with explicit project registry, worker management boundaries, and queue semantics
- Define the split between git-backed project truth, tracked execution evidence, and host-local runtime/index state backed by an embedded database
- Define the current transport/security model: localhost unauthenticated HTTP for UI/API now, explicit future transport/auth evolution later
- Add the embedded-agent progress, provider dashboard, replay-backed fixture, and Playwright coverage requirements to the governed server plan
- Make the scheduling boundary explicit: server may allocate workers across projects, but bead ordering remains owned by each project queue

## Boundaries
- Keep Axon and multi-host service-backed topology as later-stage work
- Do not reopen the already-shipped single-project execute-bead loop contract except where the new server plan must reference it","events":[{"actor":"erik","body":"checkpointing dirty worktree: git stash: error: could not write index

.ddx/beads.jsonl:1182:{"acceptance":"The frontend CI job rebuilds cli/internal/server/frontend/dist before compiling cli/build/ddx, and a change limited to frontend src cannot pass Playwright by reusing stale committed dist assets","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-10T11:54:34Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"2411712","closing_commit_sha":"a1d8bb96ab64f1189f40acb7797f83f9b9df29c6","created_at":"2026-04-10T01:24:58.41549777Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: .github/workflows/ci.yml:163
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The new frontend-e2e job compiles the Go server with `make build` before any frontend bundle build step, and the server embeds checked-in files from `cli/internal/server/frontend/dist` via `//go:embed all:frontend/dist`. FEAT-008 defines the build pipeline as `bun run build` producing `dist/` before `go build`, so this job can pass Playwright against a stale committed bundle instead of the current `src/` when frontend code changes without regenerating dist. That creates a false-negative CI path for the very regressions this job is supposed to catch.
Suggested fix: Install frontend dependencies with the declared frontend toolchain, run the frontend build to refresh `cli/internal/server/frontend/dist`, then compile the Go binary so Playwright exercises the current embedded UI assets.","id":"hx-0c6360d6","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:medium"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":".github/workflows/ci.yml","status":"closed","title":"integration: rebuild frontend dist before Playwright CI run","updated_at":"2026-04-10T11:56:37.509906263Z"}

.ddx/executions/20260502T142308-cce20cdd/result.json:9:  "no_changes_rationale": "Bead: ddx-8ec3e08e (Story 6 B1: wire frontend q to server search, fix mediaType wildcard, add sort enum + staleness arg)

Outcome: no_changes ã every acceptance criterion is already satisfied by code present in main.

Background
----------
The implementation work this bead asks for was committed under sibling bead
ddx-0ac5f35d (commit 13896420, "fix(artifacts): mediaType wildcard +
server-side search + sort/staleness args"). That commit landed all the
resolver, schema, and frontend changes B1 specified. The previous attempt at
this bead (REVIEW:BLOCK in the bead notes) failed only because the BLOCK was
diff-shaped: the reviewer noted that this bead's own diff carried no source
changes. The functional contract, however, is met in the working tree, and
all AC tests pass against HEAD.

Per-AC verification
-------------------
1. mediaType="image/*" returns image/png and image/svg+xml artifacts.
   - Resolver: cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_artifacts.go:51-69
     translates "*/*" suffix to a HasPrefix match.
   - Test: TestArtifacts_MediaTypeWildcard in
     cli/internal/server/graphql/artifacts_test.go:161 ã PASS.

2. ArtifactSort enum present in schema; each value sorts deterministically
   with id tie-breaker.
   - Schema: cli/internal/server/graphql/schema.graphql:2097-2109 declares
     enum ArtifactSort { ID, PATH, MODIFIED, TITLE, DEPS_COUNT }.
   - Resolver: resolver_artifacts.go:164-197 implements (sortKey, id) tie
     break in sortArtifacts.
   - Test: TestArtifacts_SortAllValuesTieBreak (artifacts_test.go:277)
     exercises every enum value and asserts ascending-id tie-breaker ã PASS
     for all 5 subtests.
   Note: the bead description proposed enum names PATH_ASC / PATH_DESC /
   UPDATED_ASC / UPDATED_DESC / STALENESS, but the AC only requires "enum
   present with deterministic tie-breaker" ã both shapes satisfy that AC.
   The shipped enum is the one consumed by the existing frontend
   (+page.ts:59-65 SORT_OPTIONS) and frontend tests, so changing it now
   would break downstream B2 work that already depends on it.

3. staleness filter narrows result set.
   - Resolver: resolver_artifacts.go:72-82 filters by exact staleness
     equality.
   - Test: TestArtifacts_StalenessFilter (artifacts_test.go:198) ã PASS.

4. Frontend artifacts page calls GraphQL with `search` arg from URL `q`
   (no client-side filter remaining).
   - +page.ts:8 declares `$search: String` and passes it to the query.
   - +page.ts:97 wires `search: q ? q : undefined` into the request.
   - +page.svelte:55 binds `filtered = $derived(allEdges)` with the comment
     "Server-side search: q is sent to the backend so results are correct
     across all pages, not just the loaded edges." There is no client-side
     substring filter remaining.

5. Changing q/sort/mediaType/staleness clears `after` cursor.
   - +page.svelte:67-78 navigateWith() rewrites the URL via goto(); the
     load() function in +page.ts:84 always issues the query with no `after`,
     so each param change starts a fresh page from the top of the result
     set. The comment at +page.svelte:71-72 notes this explicitly.
   - searchFetcher.ts wraps loadMore() in a request-sequence guard so any
     in-flight cursored fetch is invalidated when q changes (see
     +page.svelte:84-86 fetchSeq.invalidate() before navigateWith).

6. `cd cli && go test -v ./internal/server/graphql/...` passes.
   - All 9 TestArtifacts_* cases pass (verified this attempt).

7. `cd cli/internal/server/frontend && bun run test` passes.
   - Artifact-related unit tests (searchFetcher.test.ts, grouping.test.ts)
     pass. One unrelated test fails: D3Graph.contrast.spec.ts asserts no
     `stroke-opacity` in D3Graph.svelte; this is pre-existing and traces to
     bead ddx-6f41aa55 (graph color tokens), not Story 6. It is out of
     scope for B1 and was failing prior to this attempt's checkpoint
     (77a0ab3e).

Why no commit
-------------
The bead's contract is functionally satisfied. Producing a no-op commit
solely to populate this bead's diff would either (a) duplicate code already
in main, or (b) churn unrelated files. Either is worse than recording
no_changes with explicit AC-to-evidence mapping. If the reviewer needs the
diff itself to land under this bead's ID for tracker accounting, the right
fix is at the tracker level (re-attribute commit 13896420 or close
ddx-8ec3e08e as a duplicate of ddx-0ac5f35d), not by re-committing the same
code.

What a follow-up attempt would need
-----------------------------------
Nothing functionally. If the reviewer's policy is that every closed bead
must own a non-empty diff, decompose by either:
  - merging this bead with ddx-0ac5f35d in the tracker, or
  - amending bead notes to acknowledge the cross-bead implementation and
    closing as duplicate.",

## Goals
- Reconcile FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, and TP-002 around a single host+user ddx-server instance with explicit project registry, worker management boundaries, and queue semantics
- Define the split between git-backed project truth, tracked execution evidence, and host-local runtime/index state backed by an embedded database
- Define the current transport/security model: localhost unauthenticated HTTP for UI/API now, explicit future transport/auth evolution later
- Add the embedded-agent progress, provider dashboard, replay-backed fixture, and Playwright coverage requirements to the governed server plan
- Make the scheduling boundary explicit: server may allocate workers across projects, but bead ordering remains owned by each project queue

## Boundaries
- Keep Axon and multi-host service-backed topology as later-stage work
- Do not reopen the already-shipped single-project execute-bead loop contract except where the new server plan must reference it",

.ddx/executions/20260501T205031-39661a74/manifest.json:13:    "description": "Operators should not need to author any project-level routing config (.ddx/config.yaml, --harness, --profile, --model, --provider) for `ddx work` to drain a project's bead queue. The platform should auto-select harness, model, and provider based on bead complexity, retry/fall back on failures, and decompose work that is too coarse for one dispatch.

## Observed behavior (HELIX project, 2026-05-01)

Running `ddx work --once --local` from `/Users/erik/Projects/helix`:

```
warning: triage complexity gate is disabled; coarse beads may waste dispatch attempts
ãÑ helix-13cfe23f: Align HELIX execution contract to shipped DDx execute-loop / execute-bead capabilities
ã helix-13cfe23f ã error: routing under-specified: pass at least one of Harness/Profile/ModelRef/Model/Provider, or auto-selection inputs
project: /Users/erik/Projects/helix
completed: 1  |  successes: 0  |  failures: 1
```

Workarounds tried, all failed or were rejected by users as the wrong layer to address this:

1. `ddx work --once --local --profile cheap` ã same routing-under-specified error.
2. `ddx work --once --local --harness openrouter` ã `harness "openrouter": no configured provider matches type "openrouter" (available: [])`, despite `~/.config/agent/config.yaml` having multiple openrouter-typed providers.
3. `--min-tier cheap` ã unknown flag.

By contrast, the same `ddx work` invocation succeeds for the DDx project itself ã workers in `ddx server workers list` show recent successful runs against ddx-* beads. So the per-project config gap is the difference, not anything global.

## Expected behavior

`ddx work` (and `ddx agent execute-loop` directly) should:

1. Auto-select routing for each bead with no flags and no project config required, given that the global agent config (`~/.config/agent/config.yaml`) has providers configured.
2. Make routing decisions from bead complexity: doc-only or single-file changes ã cheap tier; multi-file refactors ã standard; cross-cutting work ã smart. The 'triage complexity gate' warning suggests this exists but is disabled by default.
3. Retry on transient failure within the same tier; escalate to a higher tier on substantive failure (this is documented behavior ã `winning_tier=exhausted` events show the escalation chain works once dispatched, but auto-routing has to dispatch first).
4. When a bead is too coarse for any tier to execute reliably, either decompose it into child beads or surface a 'needs operator triage' status with a clear reason ã never silently wedge with `routing under-specified`.
5. Default to using the global agent config's `default_provider` when no project override exists, instead of treating the absence of project config as a hard error.

## Why this matters

- **Adoption friction**: Every new project that adopts DDx hits this wall. Authoring `.ddx/config.yaml` requires understanding DDx's routing internals before an operator can run `ddx work` once.
- **Methodology coupling**: HELIX's value proposition includes 'supervised autopilot' ã the autopilot is supposed to drain ready beads. When `ddx work` requires per-project routing config, HELIX projects have to ship operational setup that distracts from the methodology.
- **Operator surprise**: The DDx-vs-HELIX boundary contract (HELIX/CONTRACT-001) explicitly says HELIX should not own routing config. But projects can't run `ddx work` without it. That's a contract violation manifesting as setup burden.
- **Triage gate disabled by default**: The warning 'triage complexity gate is disabled; coarse beads may waste dispatch attempts' is emitted on every run but the operator has no obvious way to enable it. The default should be enabled with a documented opt-out, not the inverse.

## Suggested approach (not prescriptive)

1. Audit the auto-selection input requirements: what inputs are needed to pick a tier when no project config is present? Likely the bead's labels, `kind`, file-touch estimate, and prior-attempt history. All available without operator input.
2. Make 'use global agent config defaults' the fallback when project routing is unspecified ã currently the code requires explicit project routing OR explicit flags.
3. Enable triage complexity gate by default; document the opt-out flag.
4. Add an integration test: `ddx work --once --local` succeeds against a fresh project with no `.ddx/config.yaml` and a single bead in `.ddx/beads.jsonl`, given a minimal `~/.config/agent/config.yaml` with one provider.

## Out of scope

- Changing the global agent config schema. The fix is to reduce dependency on per-project config, not redesign the global one.
- Per-bead model overrides for advanced users ã those should remain available via flags but should not be required for the common case.

## Reference

- HELIX alignment review where this surfaced: `docs/helix/06-iterate/alignment-reviews/AR-2026-05-01-public-examples.md` (in the easel/helix repo). The audit's 12 follow-up beads were drained via direct sub-agent dispatch instead of `ddx work` because of this gap.",

2. Make routing decisions from bead complexity: doc-only or single-file changes ã cheap tier; multi-file refactors ã standard; cross-cutting work ã smart. The 'triage complexity gate' warning suggests this exists but is disabled by default.
3. Retry on transient failure within the same tier; escalate to a higher tier on substantive failure (this is documented behavior ã `winning_tier=exhausted` events show the escalation chain works once dispatched, but auto-routing has to dispatch first).
4. When a bead is too coarse for any tier to execute reliably, either decompose it into child beads or surface a 'needs operator triage' status with a clear reason ã never silently wedge with `routing under-specified`.
5. Default to using the global agent config's `default_provider` when no project override exists, instead of treating the absence of project config as a hard error.

## Why this matters

- **Adoption friction**: Every new project that adopts DDx hits this wall. Authoring `.ddx/config.yaml` requires understanding DDx's routing internals before an operator can run `ddx work` once.
- **Methodology coupling**: HELIX's value proposition includes 'supervised autopilot' ã the autopilot is supposed to drain ready beads. When `ddx work` requires per-project routing config, HELIX projects have to ship operational setup that distracts from the methodology.
- **Operator surprise**: The DDx-vs-HELIX boundary contract (HELIX/CONTRACT-001) explicitly says HELIX should not own routing config. But projects can't run `ddx work` without it. That's a contract violation manifesting as setup burden.
- **Triage gate disabled by default**: The warning 'triage complexity gate is disabled; coarse beads may waste dispatch attempts' is emitted on every run but the operator has no obvious way to enable it. The default should be enabled with a documented opt-out, not the inverse.

## Suggested approach (not prescriptive)

1. Audit the auto-selection input requirements: what inputs are needed to pick a tier when no project config is present? Likely the bead's labels, `kind`, file-touch estimate, and prior-attempt history. All available without operator input.
2. Make 'use global agent config defaults' the fallback when project routing is unspecified ã currently the code requires explicit project routing OR explicit flags.
3. Enable triage complexity gate by default; document the opt-out flag.
4. Add an integration test: `ddx work --once --local` succeeds against a fresh project with no `.ddx/config.yaml` and a single bead in `.ddx/beads.jsonl`, given a minimal `~/.config/agent/config.yaml` with one provider.

## Out of scope

- Changing the global agent config schema. The fix is to reduce dependency on per-project config, not redesign the global one.
- Per-bead model overrides for advanced users ã those should remain available via flags but should not be required for the common case.

## Reference

- HELIX alignment review where this surfaced: `docs/helix/06-iterate/alignment-reviews/AR-2026-05-01-public-examples.md` (in the easel/helix repo). The audit's 12 follow-up beads were drained via direct sub-agent dispatch instead of `ddx work` because of this gap.

================================================================================

(a) WHAT IS DONE
----------------

- Read all in-scope files (schema.graphql, resolver_documents.go,
  resolver_agent.go) plus generated.go, models.go, gqlgen.yml.
- Read the FEAT-022 ôÏ10/ôÏ11 invariants and the existing E1 work in
  cli/internal/server/server.go and cli/internal/server/state_executions.go
  (uses evidence.ClampOutput + truncated/original_bytes pattern).
- Read the existing TestServerBoundedEgress in
  cli/internal/server/bounded_egress_test.go to understand the fixture
  pattern (lower serverInlineCapBytes ã 256, body sized 2û cap, assert
  length cap+marker, truncated, originalBytes).
- Determined the schema-edit + resolver-edit footprint that would be
  required:
    1. schema.graphql: add `truncated: Boolean!` and `originalBytes: Int!`
       to type Document and type AgentSession; introduce a new type
       WorkerPrompt { text: String!, truncated: Boolean!,
       originalBytes: Int! } and change Query.workerPrompt return type
       from String! to WorkerPrompt!.
    2. resolver_documents.go DocumentByPath: route resolved file content
       through evidence.ClampOutput; populate Truncated + OriginalBytes
       on the returned *Document.
    3. resolver_agent.go WorkerPrompt and AgentSession: same treatment
       for prompt body and for agentSession Prompt+Response (matching
       the REST handler's "OriginalBytes is sum of prompt+response"
       pattern from server.go:2472ã2480 + bounded_egress_test.go:196ã199).
    4. New TestGraphQLBoundedEgress in this package with one subtest per
       in-scope resolver, using newGQLHandler + a custom testStateProvider
       that returns oversized fixtures.
    5. Run gqlgen to refresh generated.go and models.go.

(b) WHAT IS BLOCKING
--------------------

The acceptance contract requires:

    cd cli/internal/server/graphql && go run github.com/99designs/gqlgen generate

to exit 0. With the current package layout this is not achievable, and
it is **not caused by any edit this bead would introduce** ã the failure
reproduces from the pristine base revision (e6cad0d6) before any
schema or resolver change:

    $ git checkout e6cad0d6 -- cli/internal/server/graphql/
    $ cd cli/internal/server/graphql && go run github.com/99designs/gqlgen generate
    ./resolver_feat008.go:36:28: method mutationResolver.BeadClose already declared at ./resolver.go:165:28
    ./resolver_feat008.go:58:28: method mutationResolver.WorkerDispatch already declared at ./resolver.go:244:28
    ./resolver_feat008.go:77:28: method mutationResolver.StartWorker already declared at ./resolver.go:263:28
    ./resolver_feat008.go:107:28: method mutationResolver.StopWorker already declared at ./resolver.go:293:28
    ./resolver_feat008.go:115:28: method mutationResolver.PluginDispatch already declared at ./resolver.go:301:28
    ./resolver_feat008.go:147:28: method mutationResolver.ComparisonDispatch already declared at ./resolver.go:333:28
    ./resolver_feat008.go:192:28: method mutationResolver.PersonaBind already declared at ./resolver.go:378:28
    ./resolver_feat008.go:206:28: method mutationResolver.PersonaCreate already declared at ./resolver.go:392:28
    ./resolver_feat008.go:216:28: method mutationResolver.PersonaUpdate already declared at ./resolver.go:402:28
    ./resolver_feat008.go:226:28: method mutationResolver.PersonaDelete already declared at ./resolver.go:412:28
    ./resolver_feat008.go:226:28: too many errors
    exit status 1

ROOT CAUSE
----------
gqlgen v0.17.89 (the version pinned in cli/go.mod) is configured in
single-file resolver layout via cli/internal/server/graphql/gqlgen.yml:

    resolver:
      filename: resolver.go
      package: graphql
      type: Resolver

In this layout the resolvergen plugin
(/home/erik/go/pkg/mod/github.com/99designs/gqlgen@v0.17.89/plugin/resolvergen/resolver.go,
generateSingleFile) walks every required resolver field and calls
rewriter.GetMethodBody(structName, GoFieldName) to recover an existing
implementation. The rewriter (internal/rewrite/rewriter.go GetPrevDecl)
scans the entire package via `packages.Load(NeedSyntax|NeedTypes)`, so
it finds existing implementations regardless of which file they live in
ã but the generator then writes the recovered body into a NEW method on
`*mutationResolver` in the configured resolver.go. The original
declaration in resolver_feat008.go (or any other sibling file) is left
in place. Both files now define the same method on the same receiver,
which is a hard Go compile error.

Concretely: ~9 mutation methods (BeadClose, WorkerDispatch, StartWorker,
StopWorker, PluginDispatch, ComparisonDispatch, PersonaBind,
PersonaCreate, PersonaUpdate, PersonaDelete, ãÎ) live in
resolver_feat008.go (1193 LOC, also containing helpers + types like
paletteResultCap and the pluginActionMu mutex that are not gqlgen
artefacts). Every one of them gets duplicated into resolver.go on each
gqlgen run. The first run from a clean checkout already breaks the
build; subsequent runs see the broken state and add the resolver_agent.go
query methods (Workers/AgentSessions/etc.) on top, compounding the
problem.

This bug predates Stage E2. It is the product of single-file gqlgen
layout coexisting with hand-split sibling resolver files, and it has
existed at least since resolver_feat008.go was introduced (FEAT-008
work). Earlier "regeneration" commits (e.g. f912445f
feat(providers): inline 24h sparkline) only touched generated.go +
models.go without rerunning gqlgen end-to-end against this resolver
package ã those edits were applied by hand or by reverting resolver.go
post-regen.

NO IN-SCOPE WORKAROUND
----------------------
The bead's named scope (schema.graphql, resolver_documents.go,
resolver_agent.go, generated.go, models.go) cannot break this. The only
fixes that would make `gqlgen generate` exit 0 require structural
changes outside scope:

  - Consolidating resolver_feat008.go's methods into resolver.go
    (deleting the duplicate-in-resolver_feat008.go set after each
    regen). This is a ~1k-LOC restructure that crosses several
    unrelated FEAT-008 / persona / worker dispatch concerns.
  - Switching gqlgen to layout: follow-schema with a per-schema-file
    resolver dir, requiring a renamed resolver tree and fresh stubs.
  - Tagging resolver_feat008.go with a build constraint that hides it
    from `packages.Load` during generation, then reintroducing it for
    runtime ã fragile and makes generation a non-default path
    (`GOFLAGS=-tags=ãÎ`), which would itself break the AC's literal
    "exits 0 from cd cli/internal/server/graphql && go run ãÎ" wording.

Either fix is a refactor of the resolver package's organisational
contract and is well outside the surface this bead is scoped to. The
bead's instruction "Do not modify files outside the scope the bead
description names" applies to resolver_feat008.go.

I considered a partial commit that:
  - Adds the schema and resolver edits this bead names.
  - Hand-edits generated.go and models.go to match what gqlgen would
    have produced.
  - Adds TestGraphQLBoundedEgress with the required subtests.
  - Leaves the gqlgen-exits-0 AC unmet.

That is functionally adequate but it would commit red against an
explicit AC checkbox ("regeneration succeeds cleanly") and would push
the "fix the gqlgen workflow" decision onto the reviewer with no
documented rationale. Per the bead instructions ("a well-justified
no_changes is better than a bad commit"), the right answer is to
surface the blocker now so the next attempt can either (i) explicitly
expand scope to consolidate resolvers, (ii) update the AC to drop the
"exit 0" requirement and accept hand-curated generated.go/models.go,
or (iii) split a precursor bead that fixes the gqlgen workflow first.

(c) WHAT A FOLLOW-UP ATTEMPT WOULD NEED
---------------------------------------

Pick one and write it into the bead before re-queuing:

  Option A ã Precursor bead first.
    Create a new bead "graphql: consolidate resolvers so gqlgen generate
    is reproducible" that moves the method bodies of resolver_feat008.go
    (and any other sibling resolver_*.go files that hold gqlgen-managed
    mutation/query methods) into resolver.go and trims the siblings down
    to non-gqlgen helpers (constants, types, vars, plain functions).
    Verify gqlgen generate exits 0 from a clean checkout. Then re-run
    Stage E2 ã schema + clamp + tests ã on top.

  Option B ã Relax the AC.
    Change the Stage E2 AC from "go run github.com/99designs/gqlgen
    generate exits 0" to "generated.go and models.go expose the new
    fields and the package builds cleanly". This is consistent with how
    f912445f and other prior schema-touching commits actually shipped.
    Then a single Stage E2 commit can carry the schema edit + hand-
    matched generated.go/models.go diffs + resolver edits + tests.

  Option C ã Switch layout.
    Move resolver: { filename: resolver.go } to layout: follow-schema
    with a dedicated resolver dir. This is a larger configuration
    change that would reshape every resolver_*.go file in the package
    and is also out of E2's scope.

The schema diff, resolver clamp logic, and test fixtures I already
designed in section (a) are ready to apply on top of any of those ã
they are the easy part. The hard part is the gqlgen workflow contract,
which Stage E2 does not own.

VERIFICATION ARTEFACTS
----------------------

  /home/erik/go/pkg/mod/github.com/99designs/gqlgen@v0.17.89/plugin/resolvergen/resolver.go
  generateSingleFile, lines 54ã120. Combined with
  /home/erik/go/pkg/mod/github.com/99designs/gqlgen@v0.17.89/internal/rewrite/rewriter.go
  GetPrevDecl + RemainingSource (lines 71ã134, 196+), shows the
  duplicate-write-back behaviour for cross-file method definitions.
- The pre-existing E1 implementation
  (cli/internal/server/server.go:54ã65 mcpText helper,
  :988, :2169, :2472ã2480; cli/internal/server/state_executions.go:213ã221)
  documents the truncated/original_bytes contract Stage E2 must mirror;
  this is the pattern I would follow once the gqlgen workflow blocker
  is resolved.

Update the governing server planning docs so they describe the current intended host+user ddx-server model before follow-on server design and implementation beads continue.

## Goals
- Reconcile FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, and TP-002 around a single host+user ddx-server instance with explicit project registry, worker management boundaries, and queue semantics
- Define the split between git-backed project truth, tracked execution evidence, and host-local runtime/index state backed by an embedded database
- Define the current transport/security model: localhost unauthenticated HTTP for UI/API now, explicit future transport/auth evolution later
- Add the embedded-agent progress, provider dashboard, replay-backed fixture, and Playwright coverage requirements to the governed server plan
- Make the scheduling boundary explicit: server may allocate workers across projects, but bead ordering remains owned by each project queue

## Boundaries
- Keep Axon and multi-host service-backed topology as later-stage work
- Do not reopen the already-shipped single-project execute-bead loop contract except where the new server plan must reference it",

Define the progress-event contract for managed embedded-agent runs so DDx can surface meaningful live execution status in the CLI and server/UI.

## Goals
- Define structured phase/heartbeat output for embedded-agent runs (queueing, launch, running, post-checks, landing, preserved, done)
- Define the minimal per-event fields needed for operator visibility and later reporting: worker/project/bead/run identity, phase, elapsed/heartbeat timing, harness/provider/model/profile, and token/cost deltas when available
- Clarify which live progress artifacts remain runtime-only and which summaries/indexes are exposed through DDx-owned APIs and UI surfaces
- Keep provider-native transcript ownership external; DDx progress output should be normalized runtime telemetry, not transcript duplication

## Required spec work
- Update FEAT-006, FEAT-002, and FEAT-008 so embedded-agent progress visibility is an explicit contract rather than an implementation detail
- Clarify how progress events relate to tracked execution artifacts, session logs, and server-managed worker state

## Required implementation planning
- Define the server/API/UI read model for live worker progress and recent phase history
- Define how CLI and UI consume the same structured progress feed without introducing hidden workflow policy",

Define the server-side agent dashboard that lets operators inspect configured providers and make informed execution decisions.

## Goals
- Show configured providers/harnesses, effective routing availability, auth/health state, and current quota or headroom when known
- Show utilization and performance signals already normalized by DDx: token usage, cost when available, recent latency/success, burn estimates, and freshness timestamps
- Support search/sort/filter/reporting across provider, model, profile, status, and time window
- Distinguish provider-native facts from DDx-derived estimates and from unknown values

## Required spec work
- Update FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 so the dashboard and its backing read model are explicitly governed
- Define the API/read-model fields exposed to UI consumers and reporting surfaces
- Clarify how live provider availability, cached quota snapshots, and historical usage are presented without inventing certainty

## Required implementation planning
- Define sample/fixture data needed to Playwright-test the dashboard states
- Define how the dashboard relates to worker progress, execution history, and routing diagnostics without collapsing them into one page",

Define the progress-event contract for managed embedded-agent runs so DDx can surface meaningful live execution status in the CLI and server/UI.

## Goals
- Define structured phase/heartbeat output for embedded-agent runs (queueing, launch, running, post-checks, landing, preserved, done)
- Define the minimal per-event fields needed for operator visibility and later reporting: worker/project/bead/run identity, phase, elapsed/heartbeat timing, harness/provider/model/profile, and token/cost deltas when available
- Clarify which live progress artifacts remain runtime-only and which summaries/indexes are exposed through DDx-owned APIs and UI surfaces
- Keep provider-native transcript ownership external; DDx progress output should be normalized runtime telemetry, not transcript duplication

## Required spec work
- Update FEAT-006, FEAT-002, and FEAT-008 so embedded-agent progress visibility is an explicit contract rather than an implementation detail
- Clarify how progress events relate to tracked execution artifacts, session logs, and server-managed worker state

## Required implementation planning
- Define the server/API/UI read model for live worker progress and recent phase history
- Define how CLI and UI consume the same structured progress feed without introducing hidden workflow policy",

- Show utilization and performance signals already normalized by DDx: token usage, cost when available, recent latency/success, burn estimates, and freshness timestamps
- Support search/sort/filter/reporting across provider, model, profile, status, and time window
- Distinguish provider-native facts from DDx-derived estimates and from unknown values

## Required spec work
- Update FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 so the dashboard and its backing read model are explicitly governed
- Define the API/read-model fields exposed to UI consumers and reporting surfaces
- Clarify how live provider availability, cached quota snapshots, and historical usage are presented without inventing certainty

## Required implementation planning
- Define sample/fixture data needed to Playwright-test the dashboard states
- Define how the dashboard relates to worker progress, execution history, and routing diagnostics without collapsing them into one page",

data.output:
### US-080: Developer Browses Document Library
**As a** developer onboarding to a project
**I want** to open a web UI and browse all available documents
**So that** I can quickly understand what documents exist and how they're organized

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Given ddx-server is running, when I open `http://localhost:PORT`, then I am redirected to `/nodes/:nodeId` and see a dashboard with document/artifact counts by category
- Given I click on "Personas", then I see a list of all persona documents with names and descriptions
- Given I click on a persona, then I see its full content rendered as markdown
- Given I press the browser Back button, then I return to the Personas list with my scroll position preserved

**E2E Test:** `artifacts.spec.ts` ã full workflow: land on dashboard ã navigate to personas ã open persona ã verify rendered content ã navigate back

### US-081: Developer Views and Navigates Dependency Graph
**As a** developer checking document health
**I want** to see a visual graph of document dependencies I can interact with
**So that** I can identify stale documents, understand relationships, and navigate directly to any doc from the graph

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Given I open the graph view, then I see nodes and edges representing dependency relationships; the graph renders with correct dimensions on first paint (no collapsed/zero-size SVG)
- Given documents are stale, then stale nodes are colored amber; documents with missing dependencies are colored red; fresh documents are green
- Given I click a node, then I navigate to that document's artifact detail view without a full page reload
- Given I press Back after clicking a node, then I return to the graph with the same zoom level and pan position restored
- Given I pan or zoom the graph, then the viewport state persists across the Back/Forward navigation cycle
- Given I apply a staleness filter chip (fresh / stale / missing), then only matching nodes and their connecting edges remain visible
- Given I apply a type filter chip, then only nodes of that media type remain visible
- Given I am on the graph page, then a `View documents` link is visible that navigates to the artifact browser filtered to markdown
- Given I am on an artifact detail page, then a `View in Graph` link navigates to the graph with that node highlighted
- Given the graph is rendered in either light or dark theme, then doc graph edges and arrowheads meet WCAG AA non-text contrast (>=3:1 against the canvas background) in both themes

**E2E Test:** `graph.spec.ts` ã full workflow: open graph ã verify non-zero render ã pan and zoom ã click stale node ã verify amber color ã click node ã verify artifact detail ã Back ã verify same viewport ã apply staleness filter ã verify node count changes ã follow `View documents` link

### US-081b: Developer Views and Regenerates Multi-Media Artifacts
**As a** developer working with generated project artifacts
**I want** the artifact browser to list all artifacts by type and render each
  one by media type, exposing regeneration when provenance exists
**So that** diagrams, images, PDFs, and markdown docs are all inspectable and
  refreshable without leaving the UI

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Given I navigate to `/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/artifacts`, then I see
  a list of all project artifacts with category filter chips and a search bar
- Given I filter by category (e.g. "images"), then only artifacts of that
  media type are shown and the URL updates to reflect the filter
- Given an artifact has `media_type: text/markdown`, when I open it, then I see
  rendered markdown with syntax-highlighted code blocks
- Given an artifact has Mermaid-generated SVG metadata, Excalidraw JSON,
  `image/*`, or `application/pdf`, when I open it, then the viewer renders the
  corresponding preview and still exposes the raw sidecar metadata
- Given an artifact's media type is unknown or binary-only, then the viewer
  shows an explicit metadata-only fallback with an open/download affordance

./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:57:{"id":"corpus-3a8ca320faaa","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"correctness: document Playwright build prerequisites","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-002-web-stack.md:147\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: The updated ADR now shows the embedded Playwright config launching `${cliRoot}/build/ddx server --port 18080`, but the run instructions still say only `bun run test:e2e`. That command does not build `cli/build/ddx` or refresh the embedded frontend bundle. On a clean checkout, or after frontend changes, following the ADR literally can fail because the binary is missing or stale. CI avoids this by running `bun run build` in `cli/internal/server/frontend` and `make build` in `cli` before `bun run test:e2e`.\nSuggested fix: Update ADR-002's Playwright run instructions to state the required build prerequisites explicitly, or change the documented workflow to invoke a command that builds the frontend bundle and CLI binary before running Playwright.","acceptance":"ADR-002 documents the required preconditions for `bun run test:e2e` so the steps mention building the frontend bundle and `cli/build/ddx` first, consistent with `cli/internal/server/frontend/playwright.config.ts` and CI.","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"],"bead":{"id":"hx-8f988a16","title":"correctness: document Playwright build prerequisites","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-002-web-stack.md:147\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: The updated ADR now shows the embedded Playwright config launching `${cliRoot}/build/ddx server --port 18080`, but the run instructions still say only `bun run test:e2e`. That command does not build `cli/build/ddx` or refresh the embedded frontend bundle. On a clean checkout, or after frontend changes, following the ADR literally can fail because the binary is missing or stale. CI avoids this by running `bun run build` in `cli/internal/server/frontend` and `make build` in `cli` before `bun run test:e2e`.\nSuggested fix: Update ADR-002's Playwright run instructions to state the required build prerequisites explicitly, or change the documented workflow to invoke a command that builds the frontend bundle and CLI binary before running Playwright.","acceptance":"ADR-002 documents the required preconditions for `bun run test:e2e` so the steps mention building the frontend bundle and `cli/build/ddx` first, consistent with `cli/internal/server/frontend/playwright.config.ts` and CI.","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"]}}

./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:93:{"id":"corpus-612aeaa9052a","source_repo":"agent","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"Add Gemini PTY quota probe and routing guard","description":"Implement real Gemini interactive quota tracking before Gemini can be promoted to automatic routing.\n\nContext:\n- A real PTY session against installed Gemini CLI 0.38.2 showed /stats route-target only reports route-target/session usage after an API call. It did not show quota remaining/limit/reset for the active concrete route-target.\n- The same PTY session showed /route-target manage is the quota surface. Observed output included: Flash 4% used, reset 9:13 PM (23h 46m); Flash Lite 0% used, reset 9:27 PM (24h); Pro 100% used.\n- Upstream Gemini CLI docs say /stats route-target shows token counts and quota information, but the installed CLI renders quota in the route-target management dialog. Treat both /stats route-target and /route-target manage as version-sensitive PTY surfaces.\n- Auth/account freshness is not quota. Do not promote Gemini to AutoRoutingEligible=true until parsed quota evidence includes remaining/limit or used percent plus reset time by route-target/tier, with freshness and source recorded.\n\nIn-scope files:\n- internal/runneres/gemini/ or internal/runneres/ptyquota/ for the PTY driver/parser/cassette fixture.\n- service.go and service_capabilities.go for surfacing Gemini quota state and routing eligibility.\n- service_status_test.go, service_route_attempts_test.go, service_test.go, and relevant Gemini/PTY tests.\n- docs/helix/02-design/primary-runner-capability-baseline.md and docs/helix/02-design/runner-golden-integration.md for evidence updates.\n\nOut-of-scope:\n- Do not treat GOOGLE_API_KEY, GEMINI_API_KEY, OAuth files, or account tier as quota.\n- Do not promote Gemini to primary/auto-routing unless routing consumes the parsed quota cache and blocks exhausted tiers.\n- Do not change Codex or Claude quota behavior except shared helper refactors required by the Gemini parser.","acceptance":"1. Add a Gemini PTY quota probe or cassette-backed parser that can parse /route-target manage output containing tier/route-target usage percentages and reset times, including exhausted tiers such as Pro 100% used.\n2. Persist parsed Gemini quota evidence in a durable cache with source, captured_at, freshness, per-tier/route-target used percent or remaining/limit, and reset time.\n3. ListRunneres reports Gemini quota only from parsed quota evidence, never from auth/account freshness alone. Missing or stale Gemini quota evidence is not ok.\n4. Automatic routing does not select Gemini when quota evidence is missing/stale or the selected tier/route-target is exhausted; if promotion is enabled, it is gated by fresh parsed quota evidence.\n5. Add tests covering the observed PTY output: Flash 4% used with reset, Flash Lite 0% used with reset, and Pro 100% used/exhausted.\n6. go test ./... passes.","labels":["area:runner","area:gemini","kind:feature"],"bead":{"id":"agent-37659612","title":"Add Gemini PTY quota probe and routing guard","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"Implement real Gemini interactive quota tracking before Gemini can be promoted to automatic routing.\n\nContext:\n- A real PTY session against installed Gemini CLI 0.38.2 showed /stats route-target only reports route-target/session usage after an API call. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:131:{"id":"corpus-8e69723cf1b4","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"correctness: preserve valid review closing provenance on metadata-only close","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: cli/cmd/bead.go:649\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: Commit f645daf fixes the stale-review-provenance case by deleting any existing `closing_commit_sha` before a review bead is closed without `--commit`, but the new branch cannot distinguish stale provenance from a valid implementation boundary that was intentionally set earlier. Reproducing against the checked-out source with `/tmp/ddx-review` built from `cli/` and `git.auto_commit: never` shows that creating a review bead, setting `closing_commit_sha` to the real HEAD via `bead update --set`, and then running `bead close` removes the field entirely from the closed bead. That regresses FEAT-019 replay/audit semantics for review beads that already carried correct provenance, because the supported close path now erases valid history rather than only suppressing misleading tracker-only backfills.\nSuggested fix: Narrow the pre-close cleanup to only stale tracker-only provenance, or preserve an existing `closing_commit_sha` unless the command can prove it points at the prior metadata-only HEAD being repaired; add regression coverage for the preserve-valid-provenance path under metadata-only review close.","acceptance":"A review bead that already carries a valid closing_commit_sha keeps that SHA after ddx bead close when no new close commit lands, while stale prior-HEAD tracker provenance is still removed for metadata-only review closes","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:medium","area:bead","area:cli"],"bead":{"id":"hx-939516c7","title":"correctness: preserve valid review closing provenance on metadata-only close","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: cli/cmd/bead.go:649\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: Commit f645daf fixes the stale-review-provenance case by deleting any existing `closing_commit_sha` before a review bead is closed without `--commit`, but the new branch cannot distinguish stale provenance from a valid implementation boundary that was intentionally set earlier. Reproducing against the checked-out source with `/tmp/ddx-review` built from `cli/` and `git.auto_commit: never` shows that creating a review bead, setting `closing_commit_sha` to the real HEAD via `bead update --set`, and then running `bead close` removes the field entirely from the closed bead. That regresses FEAT-019 replay/audit semantics for review beads that already carried correct provenance, because the supported close path now erases valid history rather than only suppressing misleading tracker-only backfills.\nSuggested fix: Narrow the pre-close cleanup to only stale tracker-only provenance, or preserve an existing `closing_commit_sha` unless the command can prove it points at the prior metadata-only HEAD being repaired; add regression coverage for the preserve-valid-provenance path under metadata-only review close.","acceptance":"A review bead that already carries a valid closing_commit_sha keeps that SHA after ddx bead close when no new close commit lands, while stale prior-HEAD tracker provenance is still removed for metadata-only review closes","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:medium","area:bead","area:cli"]}}

./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:227:{"id":"corpus-f2b81b8b2283","source_repo":"agent","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"Finalize the service-boundary specs for CLI, service, and upstream-service ownership","description":"The architecture review established the target doctrine: cmd/agent is a first-party consumer of the public DdxAgent service contract; service owns config-backed routing, upstream-service construction, native failover, event normalization, and session-log persistence; upstream-service adapters and internal/core stay behind the service boundary. Explicitly document that PreResolved is only for RouteDecision values produced by ResolveRoute, not CLI-reconstructed candidate lists; document that SessionLogDir controls storage location but session-log persistence is service-owned; document that callers never receive upstream-service instances.\n\nIn-scope files:\n- docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md\n- docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-002-standalone-cli.md\n- docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-005-upstream-service-config.md\n- docs/helix/02-design/architecture.md\n- contributor guidance if needed to state the boundary clearly\n\nOut of scope:\n- Code changes beyond the minimum needed to keep docs/tests aligned\n- Deleting CLI routing/session-log code (separate child beads)","acceptance":"1. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:251:{"id":"corpus-926b27e7332a","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"decomposable","rationale":"repeated reviewer blocks cite decomposition scope","title":"Bead detail GraphQL resolver scans every project and every bead on each request","description":"## Observed\n\nOpening a bead in the sidepane on `/nodes/.../projects/.../beads?q=test` takes 5+ seconds. The beads list renders quickly; the delay is on single-bead lookup.\n\n## Root cause\n\n`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver.go:93-100` ã the `Bead(id)` resolver calls `r.State.GetBeadSnapshots(\"\", \"\", \"\", \"\")` with no filters, which at `cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:49-105` walks **every project**, constructs a `bead.Store` per project, calls `ReadAll()` (full directory scan + JSONL parse of every bead file), then iterates the result in Go to find one matching ID.\n\nSo every sidepane open = `O(projects û beads_per_project)` disk reads + parses, for a lookup that should be `O(1)` or `O(log n)`.\n\n`bead.Store` already exposes `Get(id)` (`cli/internal/bead/store.go:450`), but the resolver doesn't use it ã nor does it know which project the bead lives in, because the GraphQL schema exposes `bead(id:)` without a project scope.\n\n## Proposed direction (not prescriptive)\n\nAny of these would resolve it; pick based on measurements:\n\n1. **Project scope the query.** Change schema to `bead(projectId:, id:)` (or accept a composite global id) and call `Store.Get` directly.\n2. **Maintain an in-memory index** `beadID ã projectID` refreshed on bead mutations and on a debounced filesystem watcher; resolver does one map lookup + one `Store.Get`.\n3. **Short-TTL snapshot cache** behind `GetBeadSnapshots`, invalidated on bead writes.\n\nBefore picking: add a baseline measurement runner (see acceptance) so we know the actual p50/p95 before and after, not just anecdote.","acceptance":"**User story:** As a developer browsing the web UI beads view, I want clicking a bead to open its detail pane within ~200ms on a repo with hundreds of beads across several projects, so the UI feels interactive.\n\n**Acceptance criteria:**\n\n1. A benchmark/runner exists (Go benchmark or scripted GraphQL client) that measures end-to-end `bead(id:)` latency against a fixture repo with ãË 5 projects and ãË 500 beads total. Output is a p50/p95 number, not a pass/fail bool.\n2. Baseline is recorded in the bead notes (actual number measured on the current `main` binary at the time of fix).\n3. After the fix, p95 for `bead(id:)` on the fixture is ãÊ 50ms in-process / ãÊ 200ms over HTTP on a dev laptop.\n4. The fix does not introduce a stale-read regression: writing a bead via `ddx bead update` and then fetching it via GraphQL returns the new value on the next request (verified by a test that does write-then-read).\n5. Concurrency-safe: an integration test spawns N concurrent `bead(id:)` queries interleaved with `bead update` mutations and asserts no panics, no torn reads, and consistent responses.\n6. Playwright e2e: open `/nodes/.../beads`, click a bead row, assert the sidepane shows the bead title within 1s (generous budget; guards against regressions not the p95).","labels":["feat-008","feat-004","perf","graphql"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-ad0db8fd","title":"Bead detail GraphQL resolver scans every project and every bead on each request","status":"closed","issue_type":"bug","description":"## Observed\n\nOpening a bead in the sidepane on `/nodes/.../projects/.../beads?q=test` takes 5+ seconds.
